question:
4. From the identity
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin 2 x d x & =\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin x d x \\
& +\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \cos x d x+\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log 2 d x
\end{aligned}
\]
deduce the value of
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin x d x
\]
solution:
Solution. Let
\[
I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin x d x
\]
[This is, of course, an improper integral which we assume for the moment to exist.] Making the substitutions \( x=\pi / 2-u \) and \( x=\pi-v \) we see that
\[
I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \cos u d u=\int_{\pi / 2}^{\pi} \log \sin v d v
\]

Therefore
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi} \log \sin v d v=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin v d v+\int_{\pi / 2}^{\pi} \log \sin v d v=2 I .
\]

Making the substitution \( v=2 w \) we have also
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi} \log \sin v d v=2 \int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin 2 w d w .
\]

Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin 2 w d w= & \int_{0}^{\pi / 2}(\log 2) d w+\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin w d w \\
& +\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \cos w d w \\
= & \frac{\pi}{2} \log 2+2 I
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence
\[
I=-\frac{\pi}{2} \log 2
\]

To justify these manipulations, note that
\[
\frac{2}{\pi} x<\sin x \leq 1 \text { for } 0<x \leq \frac{\pi}{2}
\]
and hence
\[
\log x-\log \frac{\pi}{2}<\log \sin x \leq 0
\]

Now
\[
\int_{1}^{1} \log x d x=-1-\epsilon \log \epsilon+\epsilon
\]

So
\[
\lim _{\epsilon \rightarrow 0} \int_{t}^{1} \log x d x=-1
\]

It follows that the improper integral
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin x d x=\lim _{\epsilon \rightarrow 0} \int_{\epsilon}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin x d x
\]
exists. The several substitutions employed above can now be justified routinely.

descriptive_long.question:
4. From the identity
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin 2 anglevar d anglevar & =\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin anglevar d anglevar \\
& +\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \cos anglevar d anglevar+\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log 2 d anglevar
\end{aligned}
\]
deduce the value of
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin anglevar d anglevar
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Let
\[
integralvalue=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin anglevar d anglevar
\]
[This is, of course, an improper integral which we assume for the moment to exist.] Making the substitutions \( anglevar=\pi / 2-rotatedvar \) and \( anglevar=\pi-doubledvar \) we see that
\[
integralvalue=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \cos rotatedvar d rotatedvar=\int_{\pi / 2}^{\pi} \log \sin doubledvar d doubledvar
\]

Therefore
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi} \log \sin doubledvar d doubledvar=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin doubledvar d doubledvar+\int_{\pi / 2}^{\pi} \log \sin doubledvar d doubledvar=2 integralvalue .
\]

Making the substitution \( doubledvar=2 halfangle \) we have also
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi} \log \sin doubledvar d doubledvar=2 \int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin 2 halfangle d halfangle .
\]

Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
integralvalue=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin 2 halfangle d halfangle= & \int_{0}^{\pi / 2}(\log 2) d halfangle+\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin halfangle d halfangle \\
& +\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \cos halfangle d halfangle \\
= & \frac{\pi}{2} \log 2+2 integralvalue
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence
\[
integralvalue=-\frac{\pi}{2} \log 2
\]

To justify these manipulations, note that
\[
\frac{2}{\pi} anglevar<\sin anglevar \leq 1 \text { for } 0<anglevar \leq \frac{\pi}{2}
\]
and hence
\[
\log anglevar-\log \frac{\pi}{2}<\log \sin anglevar \leq 0
\]

Now
\[
\int_{epsilonpar}^{1} \log anglevar d anglevar=-1-epsilonpar \log epsilonpar+epsilonpar
\]

So
\[
\lim _{epsilonpar \rightarrow 0} \int_{limitvar}^{1} \log anglevar d anglevar=-1
\]

It follows that the improper integral
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin anglevar d anglevar=\lim _{epsilonpar \rightarrow 0} \int_{epsilonpar}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin anglevar d anglevar
\]
exists. The several substitutions employed above can now be justified routinely.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
4. From the identity
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin 2 parchment d parchment & =\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin parchment d parchment \\
& +\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \cos parchment d parchment+\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log 2 d parchment
\end{aligned}
\]
deduce the value of
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin parchment d parchment
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Let
\[
windshield=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin parchment d parchment
\]
[This is, of course, an improper integral which we assume for the moment to exist.] Making the substitutions \( parchment=\pi / 2-snowflake \) and \( parchment=\pi-drumstick \) we see that
\[
windshield=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \cos snowflake d snowflake=\int_{\pi / 2}^{\pi} \log \sin drumstick d drumstick
\]

Therefore
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi} \log \sin drumstick d drumstick=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin drumstick d drumstick+\int_{\pi / 2}^{\pi} \log \sin drumstick d drumstick=2 windshield .
\]

Making the substitution \( drumstick=2 feathered \) we have also
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi} \log \sin drumstick d drumstick=2 \int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin 2 feathered d feathered .
\]

Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
windshield=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin 2 feathered d feathered= & \int_{0}^{\pi / 2}(\log 2) d feathered+\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin feathered d feathered \\
& +\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \cos feathered d feathered \\
= & \frac{\pi}{2} \log 2+2 windshield
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence
\[
windshield=-\frac{\pi}{2} \log 2
\]

To justify these manipulations, note that
\[
\frac{2}{\pi} parchment<\sin parchment \leq 1 \text { for } 0<parchment \leq \frac{\pi}{2}
\]
and hence
\[
\log parchment-\log \frac{\pi}{2}<\log \sin parchment \leq 0
\]

Now
\[
\int_{1}^{1} \log parchment d parchment=-1-raspberry \log raspberry+raspberry
\]

So
\[
\lim _{raspberry \rightarrow 0} \int_{rainstorm}^{1} \log parchment d parchment=-1
\]

It follows that the improper integral
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin parchment d parchment=\lim _{raspberry \rightarrow 0} \int_{raspberry}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin parchment d parchment
\]
exists. The several substitutions employed above can now be justified routinely.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
4. From the identity
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin 2 steadfastconst d steadfastconst & =\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin steadfastconst d steadfastconst \\
& +\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \cos steadfastconst d steadfastconst+\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log 2 d steadfastconst
\end{aligned}
\]
deduce the value of
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin steadfastconst d steadfastconst
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Let
\[
variabletotal=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin steadfastconst d steadfastconst
\]
[This is, of course, an improper integral which we assume for the moment to exist.] Making the substitutions \( steadfastconst=\pi / 2-rigidparam \) and \( steadfastconst=\pi-staticnumber \) we see that
\[
variabletotal=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \cos rigidparam d rigidparam=\int_{\pi / 2}^{\pi} \log \sin staticnumber d staticnumber
\]
Therefore
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi} \log \sin staticnumber d staticnumber=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin staticnumber d staticnumber+\int_{\pi / 2}^{\pi} \log \sin staticnumber d staticnumber=2 variabletotal .
\]
Making the substitution \( staticnumber=2 immutablesym \) we have also
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi} \log \sin staticnumber d staticnumber=2 \int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin 2 immutablesym d immutablesym .
\]
Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
variabletotal=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin 2 immutablesym d immutablesym= & \int_{0}^{\pi / 2}(\log 2) d immutablesym+\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin immutablesym d immutablesym \\
& +\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \cos immutablesym d immutablesym \\
= & \frac{\pi}{2} \log 2+2 variabletotal
\end{aligned}
\]
Hence
\[
variabletotal=-\frac{\pi}{2} \log 2
\]
To justify these manipulations, note that
\[
\frac{2}{\pi} steadfastconst<\sin steadfastconst \leq 1 \text { for } 0<steadfastconst \leq \frac{\pi}{2}
\]
and hence
\[
\log steadfastconst-\log \frac{\pi}{2}<\log \sin steadfastconst \leq 0
\]
Now
\[
\int_{hugebound}^{1} \log steadfastconst d steadfastconst=-1-hugebound \log hugebound+hugebound
\]
So
\[
\lim _{hugebound \rightarrow 0} \int_{hugebound}^{1} \log steadfastconst d steadfastconst=-1
\]
It follows that the improper integral
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin steadfastconst d steadfastconst=\lim _{hugebound \rightarrow 0} \int_{hugebound}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin steadfastconst d steadfastconst
\]
exists. The several substitutions employed above can now be justified routinely.

\[
lkjhgfds=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \cos hjgrksla d hjgrksla=\int_{\pi / 2}^{\pi} \log \sin mnbvcxzl d mnbvcxzl

Therefore

\]

Making the substitution \( mnbvcxzl=2 dsafghjk \) we have also

\int_{0}^{\pi} \log \sin mnbvcxzl d mnbvcxzl=2 \int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin 2 dsafghjk d dsafghjk .

lkjhgfds=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin 2 dsafghjk d dsafghjk= & \int_{0}^{\pi / 2}(\log 2) d dsafghjk+\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin dsafghjk d dsafghjk \\

and hence

\]

kernel_variant.question:
Let $\log_{5}$ denote the logarithm to base $5$ and let  
\[
\Delta=\Bigl\{(x,y)\in\mathbb{R}^{2}\mid 0\le x\le y\le\tfrac{\pi}{2}\Bigr\}
\subset\mathbb{R}^{2}.
\]

(a)  Prove that the two-dimensional improper integral  
\[
J=\iint_{\Delta}\log_{5}\!\bigl(\sin(x+y)\bigr)\,dy\,dx
\]
is absolutely convergent, i.e.  
\[
\iint_{\Delta}\Bigl|\log_{5}\!\bigl(\sin(x+y)\bigr)\Bigr|\,dy\,dx<\infty .
\]

(b)  Evaluate $J$ in closed form.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout $\ln$ denotes the natural logarithm and $\log_{5}t=\dfrac{\ln t}{\ln 5}$.

\textbf{Step 0 - Absolute integrability}  
Put $u=x+y$.  For every $u\in[0,\pi]$ the intersection of $\Delta$ with the line
$x+y=u$ is  
\[
\Bigl\{\,(x,y)\in\Delta\mid x+y=u\Bigr\}
     =\Bigl\{\,(x,u-x)\mid \max\!\bigl(0,u-\tfrac{\pi}{2}\bigr)\le x\le\tfrac{u}{2}\Bigr\},
\]
whose length equals  
\[
w(u)=\frac12
\begin{cases}
u, & 0\le u\le\tfrac{\pi}{2},\\
\pi-u,&\tfrac{\pi}{2}\le u\le\pi .
\end{cases}\tag{1}
\]

Applying Tonelli's theorem to the non-negative integrand
$\lvert\log_{5}(\sin(x+y))\rvert$ gives  
\[
\iint_{\Delta}\Bigl|\log_{5}\!\bigl(\sin(x+y)\bigr)\Bigr|\,dy\,dx
      =\frac{1}{\ln 5}\int_{0}^{\pi} w(u)\,\bigl|\ln(\sin u)\bigr|\,du .
\tag{2}
\]

Near $u=0$ we have $\sin u\sim u$, hence $\lvert\ln(\sin u)\rvert\le C_{0}\lvert\ln u\rvert$
for $0<u<1$ and some absolute constant $C_{0}$.  Because $w(u)=u/2$ on
$[0,\pi/2]$, the contribution of $(0,\varepsilon)$ to (2) is bounded by  
\[
\frac{C_{0}}{2\ln 5}\int_{0}^{\varepsilon}u\lvert\ln u\rvert\,du
      =\frac{C_{0}}{4\ln 5}\,\varepsilon^{2}\bigl|\ln\varepsilon\bigr|
      \xrightarrow[\varepsilon\downarrow0]{}0 .
\]
An analogous estimate at $u=\pi$ proves integrability there as well, while on every
compact subinterval of $(0,\pi)$ the integrand is continuous.  Hence the integral in (2) is finite, and $J$ is absolutely convergent.

\textbf{Step 1 - Reduction to one dimension}  
Because of absolute convergence we may change the order of integration:
\[
J=\int_{0}^{\pi}w(u)\,\log_{5}(\sin u)\,du.\tag{3}
\]
Since $w(u)=w(\pi-u)$, the two halves of $[0,\pi]$ contribute equally, so
\[
J= \frac{1}{\ln 5}\,K,\qquad
K:=\int_{0}^{\pi/2}u\,\ln(\sin u)\,du .\tag{4}
\]

\textbf{Step 2 - Fourier expansion and justified interchange of limit and integral}

For $0<u<\pi$ the classical Fourier series  
\[
\ln\bigl(2\sin u\bigr)=-\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{\cos(2nu)}{n}\tag{5}
\]
converges pointwise.  Rewrite
\[
\ln(\sin u)=-\ln2-\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{\cos(2nu)}{n}.\tag{6}
\]

Define the partial sums $S_{N}(u)=\sum_{n=1}^{N}\dfrac{\cos(2nu)}{n}$.
To insert series (6) into (4) we must justify
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi/2} u\,S_{N}(u)\,du \longrightarrow
      \int_{0}^{\pi/2}u\left(\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{\cos(2nu)}{n}\right)du
\qquad(N\to\infty).\tag{7}
\]

Split the interval:
\[
\bigl(0,\tfrac{\pi}{2}\bigr]= (0,\delta]\cup[\delta,\tfrac{\pi}{2}]
\quad\text{ with }\quad 0<\delta<\tfrac{\pi}{2}.
\]

(i) On $[\delta,\pi/2]$ the Dirichlet kernel implies
$\lvert S_{N}(u)\rvert\le M_{\delta}$ for all $u$ in this subinterval and
every $N$, where $M_{\delta}$ is a finite constant depending on $\delta$.
Consequently $u\lvert S_{N}(u)\rvert\le (\pi/2)M_{\delta}$, an integrable
majorant; dominated convergence applies.

(ii) On $(0,\delta]$ one uses  
\[
\lvert S_{N}(u)\rvert\le C\,|\ln u| \qquad\bigl(0<u\le\delta,\; N\ge1\bigr)
\]
for some absolute constant $C$.  Hence
\[
u\,\lvert S_{N}(u)\rvert\le C\,u\,|\ln u|,
\]
and $u|\ln u|$ is integrable at $0$.  By dominated convergence again,
the interchange in (7) is valid.

Thus
\[
K=-\ln2\int_{0}^{\pi/2}u\,du
    -\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n}\int_{0}^{\pi/2}u\,\cos(2nu)\,du .\tag{8}
\]

\textbf{Step 3 - Evaluation of the oscillatory integrals}  
For $n\ge1$ set
\[
I_{n}:=\int_{0}^{\pi/2}u\,\cos(2nu)\,du.\tag{9}
\]
An integration by parts yields
\[
\begin{aligned}
I_{n}&=\Bigl[\frac{u\sin(2nu)}{2n}\Bigr]_{0}^{\pi/2}
        -\frac{1}{2n}\int_{0}^{\pi/2}\sin(2nu)\,du
      =-\frac{1-(-1)^{n}}{4n^{2}}\\
      &=
      \begin{cases}
      0,& n\ \text{even},\\[6pt]
      -\dfrac{1}{2n^{2}},& n\ \text{odd}.
      \end{cases}
\end{aligned}\tag{10}
\]

\textbf{Step 4 - Summation}  
Insert (10) into (8):
\[
\begin{aligned}
K&=-\frac{\pi^{2}}{8}\ln2
     -\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{1}{2k+1}\Bigl(-\frac{1}{2(2k+1)^{2}}\Bigr)\\[4pt]
  &= -\frac{\pi^{2}}{8}\ln2
     +\frac12\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{1}{(2k+1)^{3}}
     =-\frac{\pi^{2}}{8}\ln2+\frac12\Bigl(\zeta(3)-\frac18\zeta(3)\Bigr)\\[2pt]
  &= -\frac{\pi^{2}}{8}\ln2+\frac{7}{16}\,\zeta(3).
\end{aligned}\tag{11}
\]

\textbf{Step 5 - Final result}  
Combining (4) and (11) we obtain
\[
\boxed{\;
   J=-\frac{\pi^{2}\,\ln2}{8\,\ln5}+\frac{7\,\zeta(3)}{16\,\ln5}
   \;}
\quad\bigl(\;J\approx-0.204927078\bigr).
\]

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  Dimension jump:  The problem is now two–dimensional, requiring skilful changes of variables and geometric reasoning about the integration region.

2.  Additional structures:  A Fourier expansion of $\ln(\sin u)$ and non-trivial series evaluation appear.  The solution introduces Riemann’s zeta function at $3$, far beyond the elementary constants of the original problem.

3.  Deeper theory:  Convergence of an infinite series under the integral sign, interchange of orders of integration, and careful justification of each step are necessary.  None of these issues arose in the one-dimensional kernel problem.

4.  Multiple interacting concepts:  Geometry of the domain, real analysis (improper and double integrals), Fourier analysis, series acceleration, and special values of the zeta function all interplay.

5.  Longer chain of reasoning:  The original integral collapses in a few lines; here one must  
   • transform a domain,  
   • identify a symmetry,  
   • exploit a Fourier series,  
   • evaluate a delicate alternating series, and  
   • convert between logarithm bases.  

All these upgrades make the enhanced variant substantially harder while still revolving around the fundamental idea of integrating $\log(\sin)$–type functions.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let $\log_{5}$ denote the logarithm to base $5$ and let  
\[
\Delta=\Bigl\{(x,y)\in\mathbb{R}^{2}\mid 0\le x\le y\le\tfrac{\pi}{2}\Bigr\}
\subset\mathbb{R}^{2}.
\]

(a)  Prove that the two-dimensional improper integral  
\[
J=\iint_{\Delta}\log_{5}\!\bigl(\sin(x+y)\bigr)\,dy\,dx
\]
is absolutely convergent, i.e.  
\[
\iint_{\Delta}\Bigl|\log_{5}\!\bigl(\sin(x+y)\bigr)\Bigr|\,dy\,dx<\infty .
\]

(b)  Evaluate $J$ in closed form.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout $\ln$ denotes the natural logarithm and $\log_{5}t=\dfrac{\ln t}{\ln 5}$.

\textbf{Step 0 - Absolute integrability}  
Put $u=x+y$.  For every $u\in[0,\pi]$ the intersection of $\Delta$ with the line
$x+y=u$ is  
\[
\Bigl\{\,(x,y)\in\Delta\mid x+y=u\Bigr\}
     =\Bigl\{\,(x,u-x)\mid \max\!\bigl(0,u-\tfrac{\pi}{2}\bigr)\le x\le\tfrac{u}{2}\Bigr\},
\]
whose length equals  
\[
w(u)=\frac12
\begin{cases}
u, & 0\le u\le\tfrac{\pi}{2},\\
\pi-u,&\tfrac{\pi}{2}\le u\le\pi .
\end{cases}\tag{1}
\]

Applying Tonelli's theorem to the non-negative integrand
$\lvert\log_{5}(\sin(x+y))\rvert$ gives  
\[
\iint_{\Delta}\Bigl|\log_{5}\!\bigl(\sin(x+y)\bigr)\Bigr|\,dy\,dx
      =\frac{1}{\ln 5}\int_{0}^{\pi} w(u)\,\bigl|\ln(\sin u)\bigr|\,du .
\tag{2}
\]

Near $u=0$ we have $\sin u\sim u$, hence $\lvert\ln(\sin u)\rvert\le C_{0}\lvert\ln u\rvert$
for $0<u<1$ and some absolute constant $C_{0}$.  Because $w(u)=u/2$ on
$[0,\pi/2]$, the contribution of $(0,\varepsilon)$ to (2) is bounded by  
\[
\frac{C_{0}}{2\ln 5}\int_{0}^{\varepsilon}u\lvert\ln u\rvert\,du
      =\frac{C_{0}}{4\ln 5}\,\varepsilon^{2}\bigl|\ln\varepsilon\bigr|
      \xrightarrow[\varepsilon\downarrow0]{}0 .
\]
An analogous estimate at $u=\pi$ proves integrability there as well, while on every
compact subinterval of $(0,\pi)$ the integrand is continuous.  Hence the integral in (2) is finite, and $J$ is absolutely convergent.

\textbf{Step 1 - Reduction to one dimension}  
Because of absolute convergence we may change the order of integration:
\[
J=\int_{0}^{\pi}w(u)\,\log_{5}(\sin u)\,du.\tag{3}
\]
Since $w(u)=w(\pi-u)$, the two halves of $[0,\pi]$ contribute equally, so
\[
J= \frac{1}{\ln 5}\,K,\qquad
K:=\int_{0}^{\pi/2}u\,\ln(\sin u)\,du .\tag{4}
\]

\textbf{Step 2 - Fourier expansion and justified interchange of limit and integral}

For $0<u<\pi$ the classical Fourier series  
\[
\ln\bigl(2\sin u\bigr)=-\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{\cos(2nu)}{n}\tag{5}
\]
converges pointwise.  Rewrite
\[
\ln(\sin u)=-\ln2-\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{\cos(2nu)}{n}.\tag{6}
\]

Define the partial sums $S_{N}(u)=\sum_{n=1}^{N}\dfrac{\cos(2nu)}{n}$.
To insert series (6) into (4) we must justify
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi/2} u\,S_{N}(u)\,du \longrightarrow
      \int_{0}^{\pi/2}u\left(\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{\cos(2nu)}{n}\right)du
\qquad(N\to\infty).\tag{7}
\]

Split the interval:
\[
\bigl(0,\tfrac{\pi}{2}\bigr]= (0,\delta]\cup[\delta,\tfrac{\pi}{2}]
\quad\text{ with }\quad 0<\delta<\tfrac{\pi}{2}.
\]

(i) On $[\delta,\pi/2]$ the Dirichlet kernel implies
$\lvert S_{N}(u)\rvert\le M_{\delta}$ for all $u$ in this subinterval and
every $N$, where $M_{\delta}$ is a finite constant depending on $\delta$.
Consequently $u\lvert S_{N}(u)\rvert\le (\pi/2)M_{\delta}$, an integrable
majorant; dominated convergence applies.

(ii) On $(0,\delta]$ one uses  
\[
\lvert S_{N}(u)\rvert\le C\,|\ln u| \qquad\bigl(0<u\le\delta,\; N\ge1\bigr)
\]
for some absolute constant $C$.  Hence
\[
u\,\lvert S_{N}(u)\rvert\le C\,u\,|\ln u|,
\]
and $u|\ln u|$ is integrable at $0$.  By dominated convergence again,
the interchange in (7) is valid.

Thus
\[
K=-\ln2\int_{0}^{\pi/2}u\,du
    -\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n}\int_{0}^{\pi/2}u\,\cos(2nu)\,du .\tag{8}
\]

\textbf{Step 3 - Evaluation of the oscillatory integrals}  
For $n\ge1$ set
\[
I_{n}:=\int_{0}^{\pi/2}u\,\cos(2nu)\,du.\tag{9}
\]
An integration by parts yields
\[
\begin{aligned}
I_{n}&=\Bigl[\frac{u\sin(2nu)}{2n}\Bigr]_{0}^{\pi/2}
        -\frac{1}{2n}\int_{0}^{\pi/2}\sin(2nu)\,du
      =-\frac{1-(-1)^{n}}{4n^{2}}\\
      &=
      \begin{cases}
      0,& n\ \text{even},\\[6pt]
      -\dfrac{1}{2n^{2}},& n\ \text{odd}.
      \end{cases}
\end{aligned}\tag{10}
\]

\textbf{Step 4 - Summation}  
Insert (10) into (8):
\[
\begin{aligned}
K&=-\frac{\pi^{2}}{8}\ln2
     -\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{1}{2k+1}\Bigl(-\frac{1}{2(2k+1)^{2}}\Bigr)\\[4pt]
  &= -\frac{\pi^{2}}{8}\ln2
     +\frac12\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{1}{(2k+1)^{3}}
     =-\frac{\pi^{2}}{8}\ln2+\frac12\Bigl(\zeta(3)-\frac18\zeta(3)\Bigr)\\[2pt]
  &= -\frac{\pi^{2}}{8}\ln2+\frac{7}{16}\,\zeta(3).
\end{aligned}\tag{11}
\]

\textbf{Step 5 - Final result}  
Combining (4) and (11) we obtain
\[
\boxed{\;
   J=-\frac{\pi^{2}\,\ln2}{8\,\ln5}+\frac{7\,\zeta(3)}{16\,\ln5}
   \;}
\quad\bigl(\;J\approx-0.204927078\bigr).
\]

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  Dimension jump:  The problem is now two–dimensional, requiring skilful changes of variables and geometric reasoning about the integration region.

2.  Additional structures:  A Fourier expansion of $\ln(\sin u)$ and non-trivial series evaluation appear.  The solution introduces Riemann’s zeta function at $3$, far beyond the elementary constants of the original problem.

3.  Deeper theory:  Convergence of an infinite series under the integral sign, interchange of orders of integration, and careful justification of each step are necessary.  None of these issues arose in the one-dimensional kernel problem.

4.  Multiple interacting concepts:  Geometry of the domain, real analysis (improper and double integrals), Fourier analysis, series acceleration, and special values of the zeta function all interplay.

5.  Longer chain of reasoning:  The original integral collapses in a few lines; here one must  
   • transform a domain,  
   • identify a symmetry,  
   • exploit a Fourier series,  
   • evaluate a delicate alternating series, and  
   • convert between logarithm bases.  

All these upgrades make the enhanced variant substantially harder while still revolving around the fundamental idea of integrating $\log(\sin)$–type functions.
